dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Cardiac Arrest at 123 W. Broad Street, Richmond.
Hi, my name is Alex, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what happened?
I called 911 because my mom was unresponsive and not breathing.
There was foam coming out of her mouth and nose, and I couldn't wake her.
Ma'am, what's your name? Can you open your eyes or talk to us?
She won't respond to anything.
Got it. Her pupils don't appear to be responding-they're fixed and dilated.
No eye opening attempted or observed.
No verbal response or attempt at verbalization.
And there's no movement in response to any stimuli.
Is there foam coming from her airway? Can we suction it out?
Yes, it's coming from her nose and mouth.
We're going to suction her airway to clear it out-will do that now.
I've started suction-took out a decent amount of foam-like material.
Can you feel for a breath or see chest rise?
Respirations: not present.
Let's fit the BVM mask and assist breaths.
SpO₂: 0%.
Let's start with BVM via mask for assisted respirations.
Mask placed, we're giving ventilations now.
Are any peripheral pulses present?
No pulse present at the scene.
Blood pressure is 0 over 0.
Skin is pale but warm to the touch, dry as well.
Ma'am, can you tell us what medications she's currently taking?
She was on heart medicine, acid reflux pills, and an inhaler.
Any reaction to medications or allergies in her past?
Not that I know of. No allergies reported in the family.
What about a medical history or previous conditions?
She had COPD and heart disease-coronary artery issues mentioned before.
When was the last time she had anything to eat or drink?
I'm not sure. She usually ate late, maybe at around nine o'clock.
On a scale of 0 to 10, she's at zero-no pain observed or reported.
Blood glucose reading: 0-will repeat if necessary later.
Let me hook her up for rhythm and monitoring-can you place four leads?
Monitor is up-her ECG rhythm is flat, no electrical activity present.
She's in asystole-no electrical activity from the heart confirmed now.
Let's initiate cardiac compressions-I've just been trained to start now.
I've placed my hands at her mid-chest level for manual compressions on the go.
CPR has been confirmed effective and is ongoing at this point.
Moving ahead with placing an airway kit to secure for ventilation.
We'll use an i-gel to secure the airway and aid breathing now.
Airway is secured-immediate breaths began once in place.
Let's switch from manual compressions to an automated CPR device.
The AutoPulse is now in place-CPR has been taken over by the device.
Need an intraosseous line-she's not responding to fluids or meds yet.
IO has been placed in the left humeral head-now secure.
First dose of epinephrine 1 mg via IO-quick push method used now.
Epinephrine has been successfully administered into the IO line already.
Second round of naloxone 2 mg directly into the intraosseous line now.
Naloxone in line-good response expected in a second.
Now, administering bicarbonate-50 mEq-ready when you are for injection.
Injection started-it's currently on course and no issues observed yet.
Skin is pale, dry, and warm. No signs of trauma or bruising anywhere present.
Pupils are fixed and unresponsive. No evidence of neck trauma.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: Persistent drainage from nose and mouth-ears appear normal.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: There is a history of GERD which could be linked to the foam drainage observed today.
Retaking BP-still at 0 over 0 as previously recorded.
SpO₂ reading remains at zero despite oxy mask being in use now.
Just confirmed-second EKG reading shows flat, no change in rhythm recorded yet.
Respirations are zero-no signs of breathing detected throughout the process so far.
Second dose of epinephrine given via IO, as per protocol.
Third dose of epinephrine has just been administered via the IO line now.
Initial impression is cardiac arrest-sticking to general protocol for that now.
Another full round of ACLS performed-no changes in cardiac status noted throughout.
Let's transport her immediately. Any preference on the hospital you choose?.
Just take her to the nearest ER without delay-Chippenham if possible.
Receiving facility staff have been notified about capnography issues and interventions continued during transport.
She is being transported in a supine position-I've locked down all straps in place now.
The capnography issue was reported to receiving, but we continue transport with what we've got now.
Retaking BP during transport-still at 0 over 0 as expected before reaching the ED.
SpO₂ still shows zero despite full ventilation during transfer-no spontaneous breathing observed.
Respirations remain zero-continued administration of BVM without return of spontaneous efforts.
Final EKG reading before ED handoff-still flat, nothing changed throughout transport.
No new rashes or skin issues-skin remains pale, dry as previously described.
No evidence of trauma or wounds observed-history assessed, no new findings.
She is taking atorvastatin, aspirin, omeprazole, and an albuterol inhaler as well.
Chippenham Hospital is the confirmed destination-Trauma Bay 2 now receiving her for care.
She's been moved with AutoPulse in place for transport stability with all equipment operational.
All straps and restraints were verified before leaving the ambulance-no history changes detected.
Starting hot transport with all critical systems online-ongoing monitoring of vitals per protocol.
Patient has a history of atorvastatin use, CAD with artery disease, and GERD-documented and confirmed.
No signs of neck trauma from airway manipulation-airway secured in a prior effort.
Additionally, she has a history of COPD-possibly contributing to presentation on arrival.
Chippenham Hospital has been confirmed as the final destination-ED team now engaged.
Patient is now in Trauma Bay 2-full handoff to ED is complete, including capnography and intervention reports.
Rechecked IO line-no dislodgement or malfunction during transport-all good upon transfer.
No complications with airway or breathing during the care and transport stages.
Transfer is now fully complete-received by ED without major incident reported.
GCS was confirmed at 3 and remains unchanged throughout the care and transport.
